title: Hashmaps / Dictionaries
description: unordered_map is your most-used data structure in interviews — master insert, find, count, iteration, and the frequency-counting pattern that appears in countless problems.

sections.title: unordered_map basics
sections.explanation: **unordered_map<K,V>** provides O(1) average lookup, insert, and delete. Declare with `unordered_map<string, int> m;`. Access with `m[key]` (creates default if missing) or `m.at(key)` (throws if missing). Use `.count(key)` to check existence (returns 0 or 1).

exercise.prompt: Create an unordered_map mapping fruits to their prices: "apple" -> 3, "banana" -> 1, "cherry" -> 5. Print the price of "banana", whether "cherry" exists (using count), and the map size.

validation.message: banana costs 1, cherry count is 1, map size is 3

exercise.hint: prices["banana"] gives 1. prices.count("cherry") returns 1 (exists). prices.size() returns 3.

sections.title: Insert and find
sections.explanation: Besides bracket access, use **.find(key)** which returns an iterator — compare with `m.end()` to check if not found. The iterator has `->first` (key) and `->second` (value). Use `.insert({key, val})` or `.emplace(key, val)` for explicit insertion.

exercise.prompt: Insert "hello" -> 1 and "world" -> 2 into an unordered_map. Use find to look up "hello" and print its value. Then check if "missing" exists using find and print "not found" if it doesn't.

validation.message: hello maps to 1, and 'missing' is not found
Use find(), compare with end(), and access ->second for the value

exercise.hint: m.find("hello") returns an iterator. Compare with m.end() to check validity. Use it->second for the value.

sections.title: Iterating over a map
sections.explanation: Use a range-based for loop with **auto& [key, val]** (structured bindings, C++17) or `auto& p` and access `p.first`, `p.second`. Note: unordered_map iteration order is **not** deterministic — use `map` if you need sorted keys.

exercise.prompt: Create a map with "a" -> 1, "b" -> 2, "c" -> 3. Iterate using structured bindings and print each key-value pair as "key: value". Then print the total sum of all values.

validation.message: The sum of values 1+2+3 should be 6 (printed last)
Use structured bindings and accumulate val into total

exercise.hint: Accumulate with total += val inside the loop. The sum of 1+2+3 = 6.

sections.title: Frequency counting pattern
sections.explanation: The most common interview pattern with maps: count occurrences of each element. Use `map[key]++` which auto-initializes to 0 for ints. This works for characters in strings, numbers in arrays, and words in text. Many problems (anagrams, duplicates, top-K) start with building a frequency map.

exercise.prompt: Count the frequency of each number in the vector {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4}. Print only elements that appear more than 2 times along with their counts, in the format "num: count".

validation.message: Only 3 (appears 3 times) and 4 (appears 4 times) have count > 2
Use freq[n]++ for counting and filter with cnt > 2

exercise.hint: freq[n]++ increments the count for each number. Then filter: only print when cnt > 2.
